title: Listening - Part 4: Writing Music for Video Games
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Aaron, a video-game composer. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My guest today is Aaron Pike, who writes music for video games. Aaron, did you expect to combine music and games in your career? || Aaron: Not at all. I played piano and enjoyed games, but I regarded them as separate hobbies. During my final year at school, friends were designing a simple adventure game and needed sound. [24]I composed a short tune for their project and was amazed by how music changed the feeling of exploring the game world[/24]. That was the first time I saw a possible job. || Presenter: How is composing for a game different from composing for a film? || Aaron: A film runs in a fixed order, so the composer knows exactly when a scene begins and ends. In a game, one player might cross a forest in two minutes and another may stay there for twenty. [25]The music has to work even when I cannot know how long the player will remain in a particular situation[/25]. That changes how pieces are structured. || Presenter: Where do your ideas usually come from? || Aaron: I always ask to play an early version before writing much. A written description can say a character is brave, but moving that character through the world tells me more. [26]I often find the rhythm of the music by watching how the character walks, climbs or moves through obstacles[/26]. Sound should feel connected to the player's action. || Presenter: What is the greatest technical challenge? || Aaron: Players may hear one section many times while solving a puzzle or searching for an object. If the tune has a dramatic ending every thirty seconds, it becomes irritating quickly. [27]I spend a lot of time creating music that can repeat smoothly without the player noticing where it begins again[/27]. It needs interest but not constant demands for attention. || Presenter: Who gives you useful feedback? || Aaron: Family and friends tell me whether they enjoy a melody, which is encouraging. But the game's designers know what a scene is meant to achieve. [28]Specific comments from developers about mood or pace help me improve the music more than general praise does[/28]. A beautiful track is not useful if it makes a dangerous scene seem relaxed. || Presenter: Do you ever use real musicians rather than computer sounds? || Aaron: Computer instruments are very flexible, but a live performer adds small variations. [29]An actual violinist can give a simple melody a sense of human emotion that is hard to produce entirely on a computer[/29]. I record short performances and combine them with electronic sounds. || Presenter: What would you like to experiment with next? || Aaron: Games are becoming more responsive, and music can respond too. [30]I want to write a soundtrack that changes according to the choices a player makes, rather than only according to the location on screen[/30]. Then two people might experience the same story with music that reflects their different decisions.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: How did Aaron first become interested in composing for games?

options.A: He attended a course about sound design.
options.B: He wrote music for a school friends' game.
options.C: He heard a game soundtrack played on piano.

questions.explanation: His friends needed sound for their game and he discovered music changed its atmosphere.

questions.question: What does Aaron say is different about game music?

options.A: The audience may spend different amounts of time in a scene.
options.B: The composer cannot see the game's characters.
options.C: Players expect much shorter music than film audiences.

questions.explanation: In a game, he cannot know how long a player remains in one situation.

questions.question: What helps Aaron develop musical ideas?

options.B: Listening to players discuss a game.

questions.explanation: He finds rhythms by watching the character walk, climb or tackle obstacles.

questions.question: What technical challenge does Aaron describe?

options.C: Composing for several instruments at once.

questions.explanation: He creates music that repeats smoothly without obvious beginnings and endings.

questions.question: What type of feedback does Aaron find most valuable?
questions.explanation: Developers can give specific comments about required mood or pace.

questions.question: Why does Aaron record live musicians?

options.A: They can play much faster pieces.
options.B: They reduce the cost of producing music.
options.C: They add emotional qualities to the sound.

questions.explanation: A live violinist adds human emotion difficult to create completely by computer.

questions.question: What is Aaron interested in doing next?

options.A: Making music reflect each player's decisions.
options.B: Writing a soundtrack for a film version of a game.
options.C: Creating games without any electronic sounds.

questions.explanation: He wants music that changes in response to choices, not only location.
